turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Gorsuch has the opinion of the Court this morning in Case 23-852, Bondi versus VanDerStok.

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: For decades, the Gun Control Act has required those who make or sell firearms to obtain federal licenses, conduct background checks, and engrave their products with serial numbers. Those mandates are designed to make it harder for criminals to obtain guns and easier for law enforcement to investigate gun crimes. This case poses the question whether the Act’s longstanding requirements also apply to those who make and sell a new product. Weapon, parts kits. As the name suggests, those kits contain parts that buyers can assemble into working firearms. Despite their purpose and use as firearms, some manufacturers and sellers have taken the position that weapon parts kits do not qualify for regulation under the Act. As a result, they say, they may make and sell their kits free from licensing, background check and serialization mandates. For its part, the government represents that this has made weapon parts kits attractive to criminals and pose a serious problem for law enforcement. Police departments around the nation, the government says, have confronted an explosion of crimes involving these ghost guns built from these kits. In 2017, law enforcement agencies submitted about 1,600 ghost guns recovered at crime scenes to the federal government for tracing. By 2021, that number jumped to more than 19,000. Efforts to trace the ownership of these weapons the government adds have proven almost entirely futile. Because of the lack of any serial numbers on these weapons, the absence of any records about their ownership and sale and the facts so many are sold without background checks. In 2022, the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives adopted a new rule designed to combat the proliferation of these ghost guns. Among other things, the rule interprets the Gun Control Acts’ definition of firearm to embrace weapon parts kits, a step that has the effect of requiring those who make and sell those kits to secure a federal license, conduct background checks, and mark their products with serial numbers. Shortly before the new rule went into effect, various plaintiffs, including kit manufacturers, filed what they described as a facial challenge to the rule in Federal District Court. They argued that the Gun Control Act does not permit the government to regulate any weapon parts kits, and on that basis urge the District Court to set aside the new rule. Both the District Court and the Court of Appeals agreed with the plaintiffs. We do not. To qualify as a firearm subject to the Act's mandates, a product must meet two statutory requirements. First, it must be a weapon. Second, in the statute's words, that weapon must be able to expel a projectile by the action of an explosive or susceptible, ready conversion to operate that way. At least some weapon parts kits in our view satisfy both of these statutory requirements. Consider an example from the record in this case. A company called Polymer 80 sells a product that it calls the "buy build shoot kit." It comes with all the necessary components to assemble a semi-automatic gun in less than 30 minutes. That kit meets the statute’s first requirement because it is a weapon. Everyone agrees that the statutory term weapon doesn’t just include working guns, but includes also some non-functional or incomplete guns like starter pistols or rifles disassembled for storage, cleaning or transport. And given that, we don’t see how Polymer 80’s kit wouldn’t also qualify as a weapon, given that it can be assembled in mere minutes. Really, when the question is whether the product qualifies as a weapon, the kit’s name says it all: buy, build, shoot. Polymer 80’s kit also meets the statute’s second requirement because it could be readily converted into live firearm. In fact, the kit demands no more time, effort or expertise to turn into a working firearm than a starter pistol does, and the statute expressly tells us that a starter pistol satisfies the statute’s ready conversion test. Because the Act’s definition of firearm reaches at least some weapons parts kits, we hold that the Court of Appeals was wrong to say that the Act does not permit the government to regulate any such kits under its new rule. For those reasons and others related in our opinion released today, the judgment of the Fifth Circuit is reversed and this case is remanded for further proceedings. Justice Sotomayor, Justice Kavanaugh and Justice Jackson have each filed concurring opinions. Justice Thomas and Justice Alito have both filed dissenting opinions.
